reason: Closed-book solvability failure: Option A asserts that a 100 K reduction at 900 K yields a substantially larger absolute risk reduction than the same reduction at 1300 K. The evidence only provides regime-dependent sensitivity (order-of-magnitude per 100 K from 800–1200 K; attenuated above 1200 K) but gives no absolute ignition delay values or quantitative attenuation factor needed to compare absolute risk reduction across zones. Thus A cannot be determined as correct from the stem plus general reasoning, making the keyed set (A,C,D) inadmissible.

MCQ; papers provided. The stem asserts that all three mixtures reach peak laminar burning velocity at a specific equivalence ratio below stoichiometric, which directly contradicts the evidence bundle stating LBV increases from lean to richer conditions and then decreases (peak at an intermediate equivalence ratio, not specified as below stoichiometric). This internal/evidence conflict makes the item inadmissible.

MCQ with paper evidence provided. The stem supplies sufficient context (lean-to-rich shift at fixed 950 K; temperature increase 950→1050 K at fixed lean equivalence ratio). Correct options A/B/C are derivable via general hazard reasoning plus explicit evidence: overpressure sensitivity lower in rich vs lean (C005), LBV non-monotonic vs equivalence ratio so moving richer can increase or decrease LBV depending on proximity to peak (C004), and ignition delay decreases ~10× per +100 K within 800–1200 K so 950→1050 K increases ignition hazard (C001). Incorrect options D/E/F are unambiguously contradicted by the same evidence/regime boundary (C001–C002) and by the general distinction between flame temperature/LBV and overpressure sensitivity. No hidden paper-only definitions required.

Short-answer item; papers provided. The gold answer’s two flaws are closed-book solvable from the stem plus general combustion reasoning and are directly anchored by the evidence: (1) NMC TR gas has higher laminar burning velocity than LFP at identical equivalence ratio, so an LFP-calibrated threshold is not conservative and can trigger too late for NMC; (2) adiabatic overpressure is more sensitive to equivalence ratio in the lean regime, so a lean-to-stoichiometric threshold can miss a region where small pre-threshold concentration increases cause disproportionately large overpressure hazard. No internal ambiguity and no contradictions with the evidence bundle.
